coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a highly sensitive and selective modified electrode for imidacloprid determination based on designed nanocomposite graphene quantum dots/ionic liquid/multiwall carbon nanotubes/polyaniline
coredata.dc:creator: Nasr-Esfahani, Parisa
Ensafi, Ali A.
Rezaei, Behzad
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a simple, selective and sensitive electrochemical method was developed for the determination of imidacloprid. To fabricate the modified electrode, at first, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with a nanocomposite consist of graphene quantum dots/ionic liquid/multiwall carbon nanotubes (GQDs/IL/MWCNTs). Then, polyaniline (PANI) film was formed on the surface of GQDs/IL/MWCNTs by electropolymerization via cyclic voltammetry at a surface of the modified GCE. Field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy were used for elucidation the GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI surface characterization. For investigation the electrochemical behavior of imidacloprid at the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI, cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry techniques were utilized. Also, the effective variables on the modified electrode such as pH, electropolymerization cycles, accumulation potential and accumulation time were studied. The proposed modified electrode showed a linear range from 0.03 to 12.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid with a detection limit of 9 nmol L−1. This modified electrode exhibited good repeatability and excellent selectivity. The proposed modified electrode was employed for imidacloprid determination in vegetable samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Imidacloprid
Graphene quantum dots
Ionic liquid
Multiwall carbon nanotubes
Polyaniline
Voltammetry

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electropolymerization of PANI on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) GQDs, (B) MWCNTs, (C) GQDs/MWCNTs. FE-SEM images of (D) bare GCE, (E) GCE/MWCNTs (F) GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs, (G) GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI (H) Cyclic voltammograms of electropolymerization of aniline on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs in a solution containing 0.25 mol L−1 H2SO4 and 7.3 mmol L−1 aniline in the potential range of -0.20 to 0.90 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for 15 cycles.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of the bare GCE, GCE/GQDs, GCE/MWCNTs, GCE/GQDs/MWCNTs, GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs, and GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI in the phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) containing 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at a scan rate of 50 mV s-1. (B) Cyclic voltammograms of the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI at different pH values (from 4.0–10.0) in the presence of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid. (C) Dependence of the reduction peak current of imidacloprid to the solution pH. (D) Dependence of the reduction peak potential of imidacloprid to the solution pH.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid in the phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI at different scan rates. (B) The curve of the peak current of the imidacloprid versus scan rate. (C) Effect of potential cycle number for electropolymerization of aniline. (D) Effect of accumulation potential on the peak current of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid in phosphate buffer (pH 7.0) at the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI.

(A) Effect of accumulation time on the peak current of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI. (B) DP voltammograms of imidacloprid at the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI at different concentrations (0.03, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.3, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0, 3.0, 6.0, 8.0, 10.0 and 12.0 μmol L−1). (C) The calibration curve of imidacloprid. (D) Influence of different species on the electrochemical response of GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI to 1.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid (100 μmol L−1 dichrlorvos and deltamethrin, 50 μmol L−1 acetamiprid and ethion, 20 μmol L−1 indocaxacarb, 5.0 μmol L-1 thiamethoxam and clothianidin).
Electrochemical determination of imidacloprid in real samples on GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI (n = 3).
The proposed method vs. the other reported methods for the determination of imidacloprid.

row.entry: graphene quantum dots/ionic liquid/multiwall carbon nanotubes /polyaniline

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a highly sensitive and selective modified electrode for imidacloprid determination based on designed nanocomposite graphene quantum dots/ionic liquid/multiwall carbon nanotubes/polyaniline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156–83111, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a simple, selective and sensitive electrochemical method was developed for the determination of imidacloprid. To fabricate the modified electrode, at first, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with a nanocomposite consist of graphene quantum dots/ionic liquid/multiwall carbon nanotubes (GQDs/IL/MWCNTs). Then, polyaniline (PANI) film was formed on the surface of GQDs/IL/MWCNTs by electropolymerization via cyclic voltammetry at a surface of the modified GCE. Field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy were used for elucidation the GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI surface characterization. For investigation the electrochemical behavior of imidacloprid at the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI, cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry techniques were utilized. Also, the effective variables on the modified electrode such as pH, electropolymerization cycles, accumulation potential and accumulation time were studied. The proposed modified electrode showed a linear range from 0.03 to 12.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid with a detection limit of 9 nmol L−1. This modified electrode exhibited good repeatability and excellent selectivity. The proposed modified electrode was employed for imidacloprid determination in vegetable samples.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA) definition, any substance that is used to prevent, destroy or control of pests called pesticide [1]. Insecticides are a bunch of pesticides that used to eliminate insects that damage plants and agricultural products [2]. Imidacloprid (1-(6-chloro-3-pyridylmethyl)-N-nitroimidazolidin- 2-ylideneamine) is an insecticide belonging to the neonicotinoids family, which has been in the market since 1991 [3]. Imidacloprid has a complete and irreversible inhibitory effect on the insect’s nervous system in the post-synaptic region of acetylcholine nicotinic receptors. The hydrophobic effect of this compound is also important for their penetration into the central nervous system of insects [4]. The residues of pesticides in agricultural products cause a hazard to consumers. Therefore, determination of the low levels of pesticide residues in agricultural products (especially fruits and vegetables that are freshly consumed) is very important. For this reason, it is crucial to develop an accurate, sensitive, easy and rapid analytical method for the imidacloprid determination in agricultural products.

As to now, a range of various analytical methods have been used for imidacloprid determination such as high-performance liquid chromatography [5], gas chromatography [6], enzyme-linked immunoassay [7], spectrophotometry [8] and spectrofluorimetry [9]. Meanwhile, these methods are expensive, complex and time-consuming. Imidacloprid is electroactive, due to the presence of the nitro-group in its structure. On the other hand, electrochemical methods have good sensitivity, simple operation, fast response and low-cost [10]. For these reasons, fabrication of electrochemical sensors has attracted much attention for determination of imidacloprid.

In recent years, the use of conducting polymers such as polyaniline, polypyrrole, and polythiophene has been developed in the electrochemical sensors fabrication [11]. Polyaniline (PANI) has unique properties such as long-time stability, large surface area, ease of preparation and high electrical conductivity [12]. Also, the numerous amino groups in the structure of PANI can act as the ideal matrix to immobilize analyst that improves sensitivity and detection limit [13,14]. There are various techniques for synthesis of PANI. Electrochemical polymerization or electropolymerization of PANI has attracted much attention in different fields owing to its advantages such as one-step production, the possibility of kinetic control of polymerization and the thickness of the film on the electrode. For example, Agilan and Rajendran presented the formation of PANI on the titania nanotube arrays surface by electropolymerization process and used it in the biomedical field [15]. Wang et al. deposited PANI on the surface of the carbon-nanoparticle film by electropolymerization and improved the capacitive performance of the carbon-nanoparticle [16]. Shih et al. polymerized PANI through electrochemical deposition method on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass substrate with the addition of graphene nanoplatelet and multiwall carbon nanotube for dye-sensitized solar cells [17]. Hui et al. modified the glassy carbon electrode with carbon nanotubes and PANI nanowire arrays by electropolymerization that exhibited remarkable performance as a supercapacitor and a sensor for the detection of nitrite [18]. Liu et al. developed an electrochemical sensor for the determination of alpha-fetoprotein based on the three-dimensional macroporous PANI by using the electropolymerization method [13].

In recent years, materials with a high surface area and good electrical conductivity have widely used in the fabrication of electrochemical sensors [19–21]. Multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have shown prominent electrochemical attributes due to their excellent electrical conductivity, high surface area, and good thermal and mechanical stableness [22]. Also, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with unique virtues such as good stability, high surface-volume ratio, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, and biocompatibility have attracted much attention in the field of electrochemical sensors [23]. Ionic liquids (ILs) are organic salts with a low melting point. The highlight properties such as excellent electrical conductivity, wide electrochemical window, low toxicity, non-flammability, and high thermal stability make ILs proper for using in electrochemical sensor [20].

In this work, we prepared a modified electrode based on electropolymerization of PANI on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with GQDs/IL/MWCNTs nanocomposite, for determination of imidacloprid. The electrochemical behavior of imidacloprid at the proposed modified electrode was studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) methods. The effective experimental variables on the modified electrode were investigated. For investigation of the analytical application of the prepared modified electrode, it was used for imidacloprid determination in agricultural products.

Imidacloprid was purchazed from Aria Shimi Company (Mashhad, Iran). HCl and ethylene diamine were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Glucose, 1-butyl-3-methyl-imidazolium bromide, nitric acid and sulfuric acid were purchazed from Merck. MWCNTs (diameter of 70–100 nm and a length of 5–9 μm) and aniline monomer were purchazed from Fluka.

All solutions were prepared in doubly distilled water. Imidacloprid stock solution (10.0 mmol L−1) was prepared in methanol and stored in a refrigerator. Phosphate solutions (0.1 mol L−1) with various pHs were prepared using NaH2PO4, and Na2HPO4 and the pHs were adjusted with NaOH and H3PO4 solution (0.1 mol L−1).

An Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (Model PGSTAT 302 N) was used for voltammetric measurements. A three-electrode cell consists of a saturated Ag/AgCl-KCl reference electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode and the modified GCE as a working electrode was utilized. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Philips CM30. Also, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were recorded on a quanta FEG 450 (FEI, U.S.).

For preparation of GQDs, glucose (1.0 g), ethylene diamine (400 μL) and HCl (200 μL) were added to 15 mL water and the mixture was stirred for 5 min. The above mixture was heated at 200 °C for 6 h in an autoclave. Then, it was cooled at room temperature. The large particles were removed through centrifugation of the obtained product at 10,000 rpm for 20 min [24].

Functionalized MWCNTs were prepared according to the reported procedure [25]. In this process, 0.50 g MWCNTs was mixed with 100 mL of 5 mol L−1 nitric acid solution. Then, the mixture was refluxed for 16 h at 90 °C. For the acid elimination, the resulting precipitate was washed with water. Finally, the washed precipitate was dehumidified at room temperature.

The surface of the GCE was polished with 0.05 μm alumina powder, rinsed with a mixture solvent of ethanol-water (1.0:1.0, v/v) by ultrasonication and dried at room temperature. For the preparation of the GQDs/IL/MWCNTs nanocomposite, GQDs (5.0 mg), IL (10.0 mg) and MWCNTs (5.0 mg) were dispersed in 15 mL water by ultrasonication for 1 h. In the following, 5.0 μL of the GQDs/IL/MWCNTs nanocomposite was cast on the polished GCE and dried at room temperature (GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs). Next, the PANI film was formed on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs by immersing this electrode in a solution containing 0.25 mol L−1 H2SO4 and 7.3 mmol L−1 aniline and performing CV in the potential range of –0.20 to +0.90 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for 15 cycles [26]. Also, GCE/GQDs, GCE/MWCNTs, GCE/GQDs/MWCNTs, GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs, and GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI were fabricated by the same procedure for comparison.

At first, in order to stabilize and activate the surface of the electrode, CV was performed from –1.20 V to –0.60 V at 50 mV s −1 for 10 cycles on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI in the electrochemical cell containing 20 mL phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). A desired volume of the imidacloprid solution was added into the electrochemical cell. Then, under the applying potential of 0.00 V at GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI the solution was stirred for 180 s and after 10 s (as an equilibrium time), DPV was carried out in the potential range of –060 V to –1.20 V. To clean the surface of the electrode after each measurement, the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI was placed into the electrochemical cell containing phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) and CV was carried out from –1.20 V to –0.60 V at 50 mV s −1 until the imidacloprid peak disappeared and stable voltammogram was observed.

Apple, cucumber, and tomato were chosen as real samples and were bought from the local market. At first, each sample was washed and processed into juice using an electrical fruit juicer. 2.0 mL of each sample and various amounts of standard imidacloprid solutions were mixed and centrifuged for 20 min at 5000 rpm. After the filtration of the top phase and ten-fold dilution with the phosphate buffer (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0), 1.0 mL of the prepared solution was added to 19 mL the phosphate buffer, pH 7.0. Then, DP voltammogram was recorded according to the above conditions.

The morphologies of the modified electrodes were studied by TEM and FE-SEM techniques. As can be seen from the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), the diameter of GQDs are approximately 5 nm. The TEM and FE-SEM images of MWCNTs (Fig. 2 B and E) show that MWCNTs have tubular structures. Fig. 2C shows the morphology image of GQDs/MWCNTs relating to the TEM. According to this image, GQDs are uniformly dispersed into MWCNTs. The FE-SEM image of GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs is shown in Fig. 2F. Compared to the FE-SEM image of the bare GCE (Fig. 1D), it can be seen the surface of GCE is quite covered by GQDs/IL/MWCNTs nanocomposite. The FE-SEM image of GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI is shown in Fig. 2G. As can be seen, the PANI coating has a porous structure that can increase the surface area.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electropolymerization of PANI on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, electropolymerization of aniline is carried out in an aqueous solution in strong acids. In higher pHs, short conjugation oligomeric material is formed with different nature. PANI can exist in three forms depending on the degree of oxidation: leuoemeraldine (fully reduced form), emeraldine (half-oxidized form) and perningraniline (completely oxidized form). Among the three forms of PANI, only emeraldin is conductive, which has a green color.

In the electropolymerization of PANI, at first, aniline is oxidized and aniline cation radicals formed. Then, the dimer is formed by the coupling of the anilinium radicals and elimination of two protons. This process is completed through chain propagation and doping of counter anion (from the acid) [27].

Cyclic voltammograms of the aniline electropolymerization on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs is shown in Fig. 1H. The anodic peaks at about 0.21 V and 0.48 V are related to the oxidation of the leucoemeraldine form to the emeraldine form and oxidation of the emeraldine form to the pernigraniline form, respectively. The anodic peak at about 0.72 V is oriented from the polymerization reactions of aniline. As can be seen, with increasing the number of scan cycles, the peak currents of the anodic peaks at about 0.21 V and 0.48 increase. This observation indicates that the amount of the formed PANI on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs increase.

Cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at bare GCE, GCE/GQDs, GCE/MWCNTs, GCE/GQDs/MWCNTs, GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs, and GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI were recorded in phosphate buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 50 mV s-1 (Fig. 2A). As can be seen, imidacloprid has one irreversible cathodic peak at the surface of bare GCE with a peak current of –1.46 μA and a peak potential at –0.967 V. The cathodic peak of imidacloprid at GCE/GQDs was appeared at -0.966 V with a peak current of 2.13 μA. This result indicates that the GQDs due to the large surface area can increase the peak current. The cathodic peak of imidacloprid with a peak current of 3.67 μA is observed at potential of –0.957 V on the surface of GCE/MWCNTs. Actually, this is because of the high surface area and good electrical conductivity of the MWCNTs. On the GCE/GQDs/MWCNTs, the cathodic peak of imidacloprid is located at –0.948 V with the peak current of 4.48 μA. As can be seen, due to the synergistic effect of the GQDs and MWCNTs, the current at the GCE/GQDs/MWCNTs is higher than that of GCE/GQDs and GCE/MWCNTs.

Also, due to the excellent electrical conductivity of IL, the peak current of the imidacloprid reduction at GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs (5.81 μA) is higher than that of GCE/GQDs/MWCNTs. As shown in this figure, the strongest cathodic peak of imidacloprid is observed at GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI that located at ‒0.939 V with a peak current of 7.52 μA. This increase in the peak current may be ascribed to the synergistic effect of the GQDs, MWCNTs, and IL. On the other hand, PANI, in addition to good electrical conductivity, has a porous structure that increases the surface area and the accumulation of imidacloprid on the electrode surface.

The influence of pH on the electrochemical reduction of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at the surface of GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI was studied in phosphate buffer (0.1 mol L−1) with different pH from 4.0 to 10.0 (Fig. 2B). As can be seen in Fig. 2C, when the solution pH was changed from 4.0 to 7.0, the peak current was increased and then decreased at higher pH. So, an optimum pH of 7.0 was selected for next experiments. Also, by changing the solution pH in the range of 4.0–10.0, as shown in Fig. 2D, the peak potential was shifted to less positive potentials, that proved this electrochemical reaction involves proton transfer. The regression equation between the peak potential and the solution pH is Ep = -0.0651 pH ‒ 0.4752, that the slope of this equation is close to the Nernstian value. This result indicates that the number of transferred protons and electrons in this electrochemical reaction is equal.

In order to specify the type of the redox process of imidacloprid on the surface of GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI, the influence of scan rate in the range of 10‒200 mV s−1 was studied at pH 7.0 in the presence of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid (Fig. 3 A). As shown in Fig. 3B, a good linear relationship was obtained between the peak current and the scan rate (R2 = 0.9976). This behavior indicates that the electrochemical reduction of imidacloprid is controlled by the surface adsorption process toward the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI.

As discussed, with increasing the number of scan cycles, in the electropolymerization process, the amount of PANI coating on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI increases. Therefore, the number of scan cycles should be optimized. In order to investigate the effect of the number of scan cycles on the electrochemical reduction of imidacloprid, cyclic voltammograms of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at pH 7.0 on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI that fabricated with various number of scan cycles (5, 10, 15, 20 and 25) were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3C, with increasing the number of scan cycles from 5 to 15, the peak current increased. This is due to the fact that PANI coating has a porous structure that can increase the specific surface area and current. When the number of scan cycles increased to more than 15, the current decreased. This observation is due to the PANI coating becomes thicker and may lead to a larger charge transfer resistance.

Because the electrochemical reduction of imidacloprid at the surface of GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI is controlled by surface adsorption, the effect of accumulation condition was investigated. For investigation the effect of accumulation potential, differential pulse voltammograms of the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI in 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at pH 7.0 were recorded in the potential range of -0.80 to 0.80 V. As can be seen in Fig. 3D, the accumulation potential does not have any effect on the peak current. Hence, the accumulation was performed under open circuit potential. For optimization of the accumulation time on the modified electrode sensitivity, differential pulse voltammograms of the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI in 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at pH 7.0 were recorded in the range of 0–300 s. The results (Fig. 4 A) showed that with increasing the time from 0 to 180 s, the peak current increased and then remained constant. From 0 to 180 s, the amount of the adsorbed imidacloprid on the surface of the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI increased and then the surface of the electrode was saturated. Therefore, 180 s was considered as the optimum accumulation time.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, differential pulse voltammograms of the imidacloprid reduction were recorded at different concentrations (Fig. 4B). As can be seen in Fig. 4C, the relationship between the peak current and concentration of imidacloprid is linear in the range from 0.03 to 12.0 μmol L−1 with a regression equation of I(μA) = 2.3211Cimidaclopride(μmol L−1) + 0.2491 (R2 = 0.9979). The detection limit, according to 3Sb/m, was obtained as 9 nmol L−1.

The repeatability of the proposed electrode was investigated by five replicate measurements for 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid using one GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI electrode. The RSD% value was obtained as 3.1% that shows the excellent repeatability of the proposed modified electrode. The reproducibility of the modified electrode was studied with five replicate measurements for 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid using five distinct GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI. The relative standard deviation was calculated as 4.2%. For evaluation of the stability of the modified electrode, the peak current of the electrochemical reduction of 5.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid at GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI was measured after 4 weeks. A decrease of less than 5% of the initial signal was observed. This result indicates the good stability of the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI that attributes to the stability of the PANI coating.

To verify the modified electrode’s selectivity toward imidacloprid, the influence of several pesticides such as acetamiprid, indocaxacarb, dichlorvos, deltamethrin, ethion, thiamethoxam and clothianidin on the peak current of 1.0 μmol L−1 imidacloprid (at the surface of GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI) was investigated in 0.1 mol L−1 buffer phosphate solution (pH 7.0). As can be seen in Fig. 4D, 100-fold of dichlorvos and deltamethrin, 50-fold of acetamiprid and ethion, 20-fold of indocaxacarb and 5-fold of thiamethoxam and clothianidin, did not interfere in the determination of imidacloprid. These results show that the proposed modified electrode can be used as a selective method for imidacloprid determination in the presence of other pesticides.

The feasibility of the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI in the real sample was investigated by determination of imidacloprid in real samples such as apple, cucumber, and tomato. In these samples, imidacloprid was not found. Therefore, an specified amount of imidacloprid was added to the samples solutions. The amount of imidacloprid were obtained through the standard addition method. The recoveries of this modified electrode are between 96.7 and 106% (Table 1 ). These results indicate that this the proposed modified electrode can be applied to determine imidacloprid in real samples.

In the present work, a modified electrode based on a glassy carbon electrode modified with GQDs/IL/MWCNTs nanocomposite and PANI was developed for imidacloprid determination. PANI coating was formed on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI by electropolymerization of aniline in a solution containing 0.25 mol L−1 H2SO4 and 7.3 mmol L−1 aniline. Because of the excellent properties of GQDs, MWCNTs, IL, and PANI, these materials showed synergistic effect toward the electrochemical reduction of imidacloprid on the GCE/GQDs/IL/MWCNTs/PANI. DPV was utilized for investigation the electrochemical performance of the proposed modified electrode that showed wide linear range, low detection limit, and excellent selectivity. Also, the proposed modified electrode was successfully applied for imidacloprid determination in real samples such as apple, cucumber, and tomato. As shown in Table 2 , the figures of merit of the present modified electrode are compared with different sensors for detecting imidacloprid. These results indicate that the PANI/MWCNTs/IL/GQDs/GCE is an excellent modified electrode for imidacloprid determination.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental and human health risk indicators for agricultural pesticides in estuaries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.T. Rodrigues, M.F. Alpendurada, F. Ramos, M.A. Pardal, Environmental and human health risk indicators for agricultural pesticides in estuaries, Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf. 150 (2018) 224-231

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Production of trans-chrysanthemic acid, the monoterpene acid moiety of natural pyrethrin insecticides, in tomato fruit

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Xu, D. Lybrand, S. Bennewitz, A. Tissier, R.L. Last, E. Pichersky, Production of trans-chrysanthemic acid, the monoterpene acid moiety of natural pyrethrin insecticides, in tomato fruit, Metab. Eng. 47 (2018) 271-278

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of imidacloprid and a neonicotinoid mixture on aquatic invertebrate communities under Mediterranean conditions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Rico, A. Arenas-Sanchez, J. Pasqualini, A. Garcia-Astillero, L. Cherta, L. Nozal, M. Vighi, Effects of imidacloprid and a neonicotinoid mixture on aquatic invertebrate communities under Mediterranean conditions. Aquat. Toxicol. 204 (2018) 130-143

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exposure to a sublethal concentration of imidacloprid and the side effects on target and nontarget organs of Apis mellifera (Hymenoptera, Apidae)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.F. Catae, T.C. Roat, M. Pratavieira, A.R.D. Silva Menegasso, M.S Palma, O. Malaspina, Exposure to a sublethal concentration of imidacloprid and the side effects on target and nontarget organs of Apis mellifera (Hymenoptera, Apidae). Ecotoxicology 27 (2018) 109-121

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and sensitive method for determination of imidacloprid residue in soil and water by HPLC, bull environ

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Samnani, K. Vishwakarma, S.Y. Pandey, Simple and Sensitive Method for Determination of Imidacloprid Residue in Soil and Water by HPLC, Bull Environ. Contam. Toxicol. 86 (2011) 554-558

Castro, Electroanalysis of imidacloprid insecticide in river waters using functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified glassy carbon electrode, J. Electrochem. Soc. 165 (2018) B431-B435.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Parisa Nasr-Esfahani received her BSc degree in Chemistry in 2011 from Isfahan University of Technology (Iran) and MSc degree in Analytical Chemistry in 2013 the same university (Iran). She is presently PhD student in Analytical Chemistry at the Isfahan University of Technology (Iran). Her research interest is optical and electrochemical biosensors.

Ali A. Ensafi was graduated in Chemistry (MSc) from Shiraz University (Iran) in 1988 and he received his PhD in 1991 in Analytical Chemistry in the same University. Then, he joined the Department of Chemistry at the Isfahan University of Technology (Iran). He became full professor in 2001. His research interest comprises the development of new electrochemical and/or chromogenic chemosensors, molecular probes and biosensors for anions, cations, pharmaceutical compounds, and DNA damage. Prof. Ensafi pioneered a new generation of DNA-biosensors for detection of DNA damage and awarded three International awards.

Behzad Rezaei is professor of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology. He received his BSc in chemistry from Isfahan University in 1988, his MSc in Analytical Chemistry from Isfahan University in 1991, and his PhD from Isfahan University of Technology in Analytical Chemistry in 1999. His research interests cover electrochemical, flow injection method of analysis and electrochemical sensors.
